summary.catchwords: FAMILY LAW – PRACTICE AND PROCEEDURE – Where the matter was listed for final oral submissions – Where senior counsel for the respondent notified the Court of potentially relevant documentary evidence created following the close of the parties cases – Where senior counsel for the respondent sought leave to reopen proceedings to adduce a letter allegedly authored by the parties youngest child – Where senior counsel for the applicant opposed any application for leave to reopen – Where the Court granted leave to reopen proceedings – Where the Court ordered the Independent Children’s Lawyer to produce all information related to the letter, not subject to privilege – Where the Court lists the matter for further case management.

fulltext:
REASONS FOR JUDGMENT

ALTOBELLI J:

This is a very finely balanced case. If it were not so, I would not be seriously entertaining what is, in effect, an application to reopen the evidence for the purposes of tendering a letter from the parties youngest child, Z; born in 2015, dated 2 February 2026. It seems to be conceded between counsel that in terms of the issue of whether leave should be granted to reopen, given the potential relevance of the document and its recency, that leave is certainly open to be granted by the Court.
The issue for the Court is the impact on the proceedings if the evidence is allowed. I accept the submissions on behalf of Mr Dura, senior counsel for the applicant mother (“the mother”), that he would at the very least need to take instructions from his client, the mother; that is Z’s mother, to provide some evidence about the context of the letter and her involvement, if any, in the letter from Z.
Those instructions might lead to cross-examination of the mother. Indeed, I anticipate that it would lead to cross-examination of the mother. I say that based on the history of this case, and without in any way knowing what is going to be in her affidavit, but this case does have a certain flavour. That is why I expect that, whatever the mother says, there is likely to be cross-examination, limited to this issue of course.
The possibility that the letter would be sent to the Single Joint Expert, Dr Honeycutt cannot be ruled out. The need for further submissions is inevitable, as well as the evidence that I have adverted to. The delay in bringing a conclusion to this litigation is an inevitable part of granting the application for leave. The delay, of course, is not in the best interests of the parties’ children, or the parents, or indeed from the Court’s perspective, an ideal situation. But this Court is clearly charged, under the Family Law Act 1975 (Cth), with making orders that promote the safety of children.
As I said before, this is a finely balanced case. The risk issues are nuanced. On balance, and not without some hesitation, I am going to grant the application to reopen, and allow the tender of the document dated 2 February 2026, purporting to be a letter from Z. That document will become a tender in the father's case and will thus become Exhibit R40.
One condition of the tender, however, is that the Independent Children's Lawyer tender or cause to be tendered, any evidence of the communication from, or with Z, related to this particular letter, and subject to any objection that might be made about privilege or otherwise. I impose that condition, as I think the Court needs to have the complete context of the letter. Subject to privilege issues, I think those further documents will provide some context.
